- This discovery significantly lowers the experimental threshold for achieving **quantum advantage** in distributed decision-making frameworks.
key_results: Demonstration that entanglement is not a mandatory requirement for quantum advantage in Bayesian games.
Identification of specific game scenarios where separable quantum states outperform all classical strategies.
Validation of non-classical correlations as a distinct resource for independent players in classical environments.
Reduction in the hardware and state-preparation complexity required to realize quantum-enhanced game theory.
Extension of the theory of quantum equilibria to include discord-based states rather than just entangled ones.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers prove that separable quantum states enable superior equilibria in Bayesian games, outperforming all possible classical correlations.

title: ProofCouncil: An LLM Agent for Solving Open Mathematical Problems
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown increasing promise in solving open problems in mathematics. However, their performance can be further improved through agentic workflows tailored to real-world mathematical practice. To this end, we introduce ProofCouncil, a mathematical agent that is designed to tackle open problems using an author-critic architecture. ProofCouncil served as a submission to the second batch of FirstProof, a challenge consisting of 10 real-world mathematical problems that agents must solve autonomously. Its submissions for 6 of the 10 problems were judged by the referees to be correct up to at most minor revisions, showing the best performance among participating teams. We also evaluate ProofCouncil on 30 open problems collected from mathematical researchers. Among the 21 solutions that received human feedback, 5 were judged completely correct, 2 more were judged promising pending final verification, and a further 8 contained useful partial progress. In this short paper, we describe the development of ProofCouncil and the agent-building library used to create it, which we release as open source to the community.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **ProofCouncil**, a specialized LLM agent employing an **author-critic architecture** to solve open-ended, real-world mathematical problems.
- Implements an **agentic workflow** designed to mimic human mathematical practice, utilizing iterative drafting, rigorous verification, and feedback loops.
- Releases an **open-source agent-building library** alongside the model to facilitate community development of domain-specific mathematical agents.
- Demonstrates the efficacy of **Reasoning Models** in autonomous research settings, outperforming other teams in competitive mathematical benchmarks.
key_results: Successfully solved 6 out of 10 problems in the FirstProof challenge with high accuracy.
Achieved the best performance among all participating teams in the autonomous math challenge.
Generated 5 completely correct solutions for 30 open problems provided by math researchers.
Produced 8 instances of useful partial progress on unsolved mathematical research problems.
Validated the author-critic framework as a viable method for tackling complex reasoning tasks without human intervention.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ProofCouncil researchers develop an author-critic agentic workflow that outperforms competitors in solving open-world mathematical problems autonomously.

title: Report on the Advanced Linear Collider Study Group (ALEGRO) Workshop 2026
summary:
The 7th ALEGRO workshop, hosted by INFN Frascati National Laboratories from 3rd to 5th March 2026, brought together the international Advanced Novel Accelerators (ANA) community to discuss the role of advanced wakefield accelerators (AWA) in particle and high-energy physics. Organized under the ICFA-ANA panel, the workshop highlighted recent progress in plasma- and structure-based wakefield acceleration and strengthened international collaboration toward future energy-frontier colliders.
  A major focus was the ongoing 10 TeV linear collider design study, launched in 2025 following the US P5 recommendations. A dedicated session covered accelerator concepts, enabling technologies, and the associated physics case, while recognizing that future priorities will depend on the outcome of the European Strategy for Particle Physics Update.
  The workshop also reviewed nearer-term applications of advanced accelerators, including fixed-target experiments, injectors for future colliders and light sources, plasma-based Higgs factory concepts, and proton-driven plasma wakefield acceleration. Beyond high-energy physics, sessions covered free-electron lasers, synchrotron light sources, and strong-field QED. Recent demonstrations of FEL lasing with plasma-accelerated electron beams highlighted significant progress in beam quality and accelerator performance.
  Operational challenges for reliable user facilities, including high-availability laser and electron-beam systems, were discussed alongside the growing role of artificial intelligence and machine learning for accelerator optimization and control.
  This report summarizes the workshop discussions and conclusions from the chairs, together with short contributions from the presenters, providing an overview of the current status and future prospects of advanced wakefield accelerators.

processed.summary:
• Exploration of **Advanced Wakefield Acceleration (AWA)** architectures, focusing on plasma- and structure-based methods for next-generation **10 TeV linear colliders**.
• Increasing integration of **Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning** into accelerator control loops to enhance the reliability and optimization of high-availability beam systems.
• Development of **Higgs factory** concepts and FEL applications, leveraging recent breakthroughs in plasma-accelerated electron beam quality and stability.
key_results: Initiation of a dedicated 10 TeV linear collider design study following US P5 recommendations.
Successful demonstration of FEL lasing using plasma-accelerated electron beams.
Adoption of ML-driven frameworks for precision control and optimization of accelerator facilities.
Progress in proton-driven plasma wakefield acceleration for high-energy physics applications.
Drafting of international roadmaps for energy-frontier colliders ahead of the European Strategy Update.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ALEGRO researchers explore advanced wakefield accelerators and integrate AI/ML for autonomous beam optimization and facility control.

title: Active rejection enables reliable generalization of universal machine-learning interatomic potentials
summary: Universal machine learning interatomic potentials (uMLIPs) bridge quantum-mechanical accuracy and large-scale molecular dynamics, but the cost of high-accuracy calculations such as r$^2$SCAN limits training to datasets that remain small relative to the open materials space. Strong average benchmark performance also does not guarantee reliable energy--force predictions for every structure. We propose Adaptive Multi-Teacher Routing (ATR), which reformulates high-fidelity data construction as a structure-wise decision problem under uncertainty. Using a small set of real r$^2$SCAN labels, ATR calibrates multiple pretrained uMLIP teachers and combines structural descriptors, teacher identity, and inter-teacher disagreement to estimate the reliability of each structure--teacher pair. It selects high-confidence predictions for pseudo-label generation and rejects structures for which no teacher is sufficiently reliable. With real r$^2$SCAN labels for only 0.2\% of candidate structures, ATR distils 2.89 million traceable r$^2$SCAN-level pseudo-labels for pretraining. On held-out r$^2$SCAN structures and the MP-r$^2$SCAN benchmark, a lightweight CHGNet trained on the ATR-generated dataset consistently outperforms the baseline and non-routed controls. Finite-temperature molecular dynamics further shows that ATR improves dynamical robustness across multiple material systems, maintaining stable trajectories where baseline simulations undergo catastrophic structural collapse. These results establish active rejection as an effective mechanism for converting multiple pretrained uMLIPs into a scalable and reliable data-construction system for high-fidelity uMLIPs.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **Adaptive Multi-Teacher Routing (ATR)**, a framework that reformulates high-fidelity data construction for interatomic potentials as a decision problem under uncertainty.
* Utilizes **active rejection** to filter out unreliable molecular structures by analyzing inter-teacher disagreement and structural descriptors among multiple pretrained **uMLIP teachers**.
* Distills 2.89 million high-accuracy **r2SCAN-level pseudo-labels** from a minimal set of real labels (0.2%), enabling the training of lightweight models like **CHGNet**.
* Enhances **dynamical robustness** in molecular dynamics simulations, successfully preventing the catastrophic structural collapse observed in baseline universal machine-learning interatomic potentials.
key_results: Successfully generated 2.89 million traceable r2SCAN-level pseudo-labels using only 0.2% real candidate structures.
The ATR-trained CHGNet consistently outperformed baseline and non-routed controls on the MP-r2SCAN benchmark.
Demonstrated significantly improved stability in finite-temperature molecular dynamics across diverse material systems.
Validated active rejection as a scalable mechanism for high-fidelity data construction in materials science ML.
Achieved higher energy and force prediction accuracy on held-out r2SCAN structure sets compared to standard uMLIPs.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: UC Berkeley researchers introduce ATR to distill high-fidelity interatomic potentials via active rejection, significantly improving molecular simulation reliability.

title: Practical Source Code Recovery from Binary Functions Using Anchor-Based Retrieval and LLM Reasoning
summary: We present a practical pipeline for recovering source code from stripped binary functions by combining reverse engineering, anchor-based source code retrieval, and large language model reasoning. Our binary-to-source-code retrieval method attempts to identify the source function from a source code database, rather than generating approximate decompiled pseudocode. It extracts anchors such as strings, constants, external calls, and available function names using Ghidra, retrieves candidate files via an inverted-index search database, narrows candidates to likely function snippets, and re-ranks them with a large language model (LLM) based on disassembly, decompiled code, and source metadata. Confident matches can also serve as anchors in later passes. In an evaluation backed by our high-fidelity source code database on a stripped, optimized tcpdump binary, our proposed binary-to-source matching method achieves 95.2% assembly instruction coverage. Experiments on a GitHub-based retrieval database showed lower performance with 35.5% instruction coverage on average, mainly due to retrieval misses. These results show that source-level binary recovery excels with high-quality databases and remains a useful tool in noisy environments.

processed.summary:
* Proposes a **multi-stage pipeline** for binary function recovery that combines **anchor-based retrieval** with LLM re-ranking to match stripped binaries to original source code.
* Utilizes **Ghidra** to extract symbolic anchors such as strings, constants, and external calls, feeding them into an **inverted-index search** for candidate identification.
* Employs **LLM reasoning** to perform final semantic validation and re-ranking, comparing low-level assembly/decompilation against high-level source metadata.
* Features an **iterative matching strategy** where newly recovered functions act as anchors for subsequent passes, increasing coverage in complex, stripped binaries.
key_results: Achieved 95.2% assembly instruction coverage on a stripped, optimized tcpdump binary using a high-fidelity database.
Recorded 35.5% average coverage when using a noisier, broader GitHub-based retrieval database.
Demonstrated that LLM-based re-ranking significantly improves precision over keyword-only retrieval methods.
Validated the effectiveness of using constants and strings as high-confidence anchors for binary-source mapping.
Showcased that exact source retrieval is a viable alternative to generative pseudocode for reverse engineering tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop a pipeline using LLM reasoning and anchor-based retrieval to recover 95.2% of source code from binaries.

title: Diagnosing quantum reservoirs at scale based on expressivity and coverage
summary: Quantum reservoirs offer a hardware-friendly route to quantum machine learning, replacing trainable circuits with fixed random dynamics and a classical readout. Because the reservoir is not optimized, performance depends entirely on the choice of reservoir family, yet existing diagnostics demand resources that grow exponentially with system size. We introduce a scalable, hardware-agnostic framework built on two complementary quantities. The first is a task-independent order-statistics (ORS) expressivity score, which compares only the largest output probabilities of a reservoir ensemble against an analytical Haar baseline. It never reconstructs the full output distribution, is cost-independent of Hilbert-space dimension, and admits a closed-form depolarizing noise correction, making it directly usable on hardware. The second is the task-dependent effective rank $R_{\mathrm{eff}}$ of the feature matrix, which measures how much input-dependent information reaches the readout. We validate the ORS score against established complexity diagnostics and confirm it remains informative under simulated noise and on IBM quantum hardware. Across synthetic and real quantum extreme learning machine and quantum reservoir computing benchmarks, ORS captures the intrinsic expressivity hierarchy of reservoir families while $R_{\mathrm{eff}}$ determines when that expressivity becomes usable predictive information.

processed.summary:
• Introduces a scalable diagnostic framework for **Quantum Reservoir Computing (QRC)** and **Quantum Extreme Learning Machines (QELM)** to address the exponential scaling bottleneck of traditional metrics.
• Proposes the **Order-Statistics (ORS) expressivity score**, a task-independent metric that compares top output probabilities against a Haar baseline without reconstructing the full quantum state.
• Utilizes **Effective Rank (R_eff)** as a task-dependent coverage measure to quantify the amount of input-dependent information that successfully reaches the classical readout.
• Demonstrates a **noise-robust evaluation** method that includes a closed-form depolarizing noise correction, making it functional on real NISQ hardware like IBM systems.
key_results: The ORS score provides a task-independent expressivity hierarchy that matches established complexity diagnostics while scaling efficiently.
Effective Rank (R_eff) identifies the boundary where high expressivity translates into usable predictive information for ML tasks.
The framework successfully diagnosed reservoir performance on IBM quantum hardware under real-world noise conditions.
Eliminated the need for Hilbert-space dimension-dependent costs in evaluating reservoir families.
Validated performance across both synthetic and real-world quantum machine learning benchmarks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a scalable diagnostic framework for quantum reservoirs to evaluate expressivity and information coverage without exponential overhead.

title: Test-Time Scaling for Small VLMs on Multilingual Visual MCQ
summary: Test-time scaling (TTS) reliably improves reasoning in large language models, but whether it transfers to small open vision-language models remains unclear. We examine this on EXAMS-V, a multilingual visual multiple-choice benchmark, comparing self-consistency, describe-then-reason with PRM-guided beam search, and two post-hoc selectors across Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct and Qwen3.5-4B. What matters is the conditions under which TTS runs, not the search or verification machinery. The largest factor is parseability: an early prompt format left many chains reasoning correctly yet never committing to an answer letter, which a standard answer cue and a guided repair step largely remove. A larger decoding budget removes the rest: raising the per-chain token limit from 1k to 2k recovers 3.7 pp, whereas sampling more chains (8 to 16) adds only 0.15 pp. Once chains have room to finish, elaborate methods contribute little: PRM-guided beam search trails plain self-consistency by 0.39 pp at over eight times the cost, and neither a training-free generative critic nor a trained multimodal PRM beats majority vote across both policies. The largest gain comes instead from the policy model itself (+11.4 pp). Our best configuration reaches 84.1% on the held-out ImageCLEF 2026 test split, ranking first on the Visual MCQ leaderboard.

processed.summary:
* Investigates **Test-Time Scaling (TTS)** strategies for small Vision-Language Models (VLMs) like Qwen2.5-VL on the multilingual **EXAMS-V** benchmark.
* Finds that **parseability** and **token decoding limits** are the primary bottlenecks for reasoning performance rather than search or verification logic.
* Demonstrates that **Majority Vote (Self-Consistency)** consistently outperforms complex **PRM-guided beam search** and generative critics in both cost and accuracy.
* Highlights that increasing the **decoding budget** (2k vs 1k tokens) provides significantly higher gains than increasing the number of sampled chains.
key_results: Increasing per-chain token limit from 1k to 2k recovered 3.7 percentage points in accuracy.
Raising sampling count from 8 to 16 chains provided a negligible gain of only 0.15 percentage points.
PRM-guided beam search was 0.39 percentage points less accurate than simple self-consistency while being 8x more expensive.
Base policy model improvements accounted for the largest overall performance jump of 11.4 percentage points.
Achieved 84.1% on the ImageCLEF 2026 test split, ranking first on the Visual MCQ leaderboard.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that token limits and model quality outweigh complex search algorithms for scaling small VLM reasoning.

title: How Do Software Professionals Evaluate AI-Generated Code? (Registered Report)
summary: Recent advances in generative AI tools have significantly changed how software professionals write, evaluate, and interact with code. Generative AI tools such as GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT, and Claude are increasingly being integrated into everyday workflows. Despite the growing adoption of and reliance on these tools, it remains unclear as to how software professionals evaluate the code they generate. To explore this topic, we will conduct a constructivist grounded theory study that incorporates a survey, semi-structured interviews, and laddering interviews. With the initial survey data collection complete, we aim to interview 20--50 software professionals iteratively until theoretical saturation is achieved. This research aims to build a theory of how software professionals evaluate AI-generated code, grounded in their accounts of evaluative practices, perceptions, and preferences.

processed.summary:
• This **registered report** details a qualitative methodology for investigating how software professionals assess and validate **AI-generated code**.
• The study utilizes **constructivist grounded theory**, combining surveys and **laddering interviews** to elicit the cognitive processes and value judgments of developers.
• By focusing on tools like **GitHub Copilot and Claude**, the research aims to establish a theoretical framework for **human-centric AI evaluation** in software engineering.
• The outcome will guide the development of future **generative AI tools** by aligning model outputs with professional standards and trust requirements.
key_results: Establishment of a rigorous qualitative framework for studying AI-assisted coding workflows.
Completion of initial survey data collection as a foundation for deep-dive semi-structured interviews.
Targeting theoretical saturation via iterative interviews with 20 to 50 software industry professionals.
Investigation into how developer perceptions of reliability impact the adoption of LLM-generated code.
Mapping the evolution of software verification practices in response to integrated generative AI tools.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers are developing a grounded theory to explain how software professionals evaluate code generated by AI models.

title: comprisk: A scikit-learn-compatible Python toolkit for competing-risks survival analysis
summary: Medical time-to-event data are frequently subject to competing risks, where the occurrence of one terminal event precludes the others and standard survival methods that treat competing events as censoring yield biased absolute-risk estimates. Correct analysis instead targets the cause-specific cumulative incidence function (CIF). This methodology has been available to applied researchers almost exclusively through R packages, forcing Python-based machine-learning workflows into a Python-to-R round trip. We present comprisk, a scikit-learn-compatible Python toolkit that consolidates the canonical competing-risks methods (a scalable competing-risks random survival forest together with Fine-Gray subdistribution-hazard regression including a penalized variant, cause-specific Cox regression, the Aalen-Johansen CIF estimator, and Gray's K-sample test) behind a single, consistent API, and adds competing-risks-aware model evaluation (inverse probability of censoring weighted time-dependent AUC and Brier score, cause-specific concordance indices with closed-form confidence intervals, and calibration curves). Every estimator is validated numerically against the established R reference implementations. The forest uses a histogram-based, numba-compiled split kernel that fits 10-22x faster than randomForestSRC at comparable discrimination on real electronic-health-record cohorts and scales to n = 10^6 on a consumer CPU. comprisk is distributed on PyPI and lets applied researchers perform correct and scalable competing-risks analysis entirely within the Python scientific stack.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **comprisk**, a Python-native toolkit that addresses the lack of high-performance **competing-risks survival analysis** tools in the Python scientific stack.
* Features a **scikit-learn-compatible** architecture, providing a unified API for Random Survival Forests, Fine-Gray regression, and Aalen-Johansen CIF estimators.
* Leverages a **numba-compiled split kernel** and histogram-based algorithms to achieve significant computational efficiency over legacy R-based implementations.
* Integrates **competing-risks-aware evaluation** metrics, such as IPCW time-dependent AUC and Brier scores, for robust model validation on large EHR datasets.
key_results: Achieves 10-22x faster training speeds compared to the standard R randomForestSRC package.
Scales to datasets with 1,000,000 samples on standard consumer CPUs.
Provides numerical parity with established R reference implementations for all core estimators.
Implements cause-specific concordance indices with closed-form confidence intervals.
Consolidates Fine-Gray subdistribution-hazard regression and penalized variants into a single Python library.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce comprisk, a high-performance Python toolkit that accelerates competing-risks survival analysis by up to 22x.

title: Multimodal Scenario Similarity Search for Autonomous Driving
summary: Large-scale autonomous-driving datasets contain vast numbers of recorded scenarios, creating a need for efficient retrieval methods that can identify situations similar to a given query. Existing approaches typically rely on either visual representations or motion-based descriptions, making it difficult to understand their relative strengths and limitations for scenario retrieval. In this work, we present a multimodal framework for autonomous-driving scenario retrieval that combines visual and trajectory-based representations within a unified retrieval pipeline. We investigate two trajectory-based approaches: Exo-Trajectory, an explicit matching method based on surrounding-agent motion, and ScenarioFormer, a transformer-based representation learned from object trajectories using contrastive learning. We compare these approaches against strong vision-based baselines and analyze their behavior across a diverse set of driving scenarios. Experimental results show that trajectory representations provide strong retrieval performance for motion-centric events such as cut-ins, turning maneuvers, and traffic queueing, while visual embeddings excel when appearance cues are informative. Most importantly, combining visual and trajectory information consistently improves retrieval quality, yielding the best overall performance. These findings demonstrate that appearance and motion capture are complementary notions of scenario similarity and motivate multimodal retrieval systems for autonomous-driving data mining, dataset curation, and scenario-based validation.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a **multimodal retrieval framework** for autonomous driving that integrates visual embeddings with **trajectory-based representations** for scenario search.
• Features **ScenarioFormer**, a transformer-based model trained using **contrastive learning** to capture latent motion patterns of agents.
• Incorporates **Exo-Trajectory**, an explicit matching approach for surrounding agent motion to provide a baseline for geometric similarity.
• Demonstrates that a unified pipeline leveraging both **appearance and motion** features achieves superior retrieval accuracy over unimodal methods.
key_results: Trajectory representations excel in motion-specific events such as cut-ins and turning maneuvers.
Vision-based embeddings are more effective for scenarios where environmental appearance cues are informative.
Combining visual and trajectory information consistently yields the highest overall retrieval performance.
ScenarioFormer provides a robust learned representation for object trajectories through contrastive objectives.
The framework facilitates automated dataset curation and scenario-based validation for autonomous driving stacks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a multimodal framework combining ScenarioFormer and visual embeddings to significantly improve autonomous driving scenario retrieval.

title: Action-Factored Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Scalable Quantum Device Tuning
summary: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning is well suited to problems with large parameter spaces and exploitable local structure, such as the tuning of electrostatically-defined quantum-dot arrays. However, if parameter cross-talk is strong, a non-stationary environment from the perspective of any individual agent can destabilize learning - the same effect that plagues manual tuning of such systems. We propose using a factored representation of the action space, learned online, to decouple agents and minimize their interference. Our framework, QADAPT, uses this factorization to efficiently learn shared policies based on local measurements and rewards. With this modular strategy, we achieve zero-shot generalization to unseen quantum device sizes and maintain an approximately constant number of convergence steps to reach target regimes. This work provides a scalable route toward the rapid calibration of large-scale quantum processors.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **QADAPT**, a cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework designed for tuning high-dimensional **quantum-dot arrays**.
- Implements an **action-factored representation** learned online to decouple agent interactions and mitigate the destabilizing effects of **parameter cross-talk**.
- Employs a **modular policy strategy** that utilizes local measurements and rewards to facilitate efficient training across shared agent policies.
- Demonstrates a scalable path for **quantum device calibration** by maintaining constant convergence complexity relative to device size.
key_results: Successfully decoupled agent interference through learned action factorization.
Achieved zero-shot generalization to larger, unseen quantum device architectures.
Maintained a near-constant number of convergence steps across various device scales.
Reduced environmental non-stationarity that typically plagues manual and standard RL tuning.
Validated a modular strategy for the rapid calibration of large-scale quantum processors.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce QADAPT, a factored multi-agent RL framework enabling scalable, zero-shot calibration of complex quantum processors.

title: PesTwin: A modular agent-based framework for pest and vector population control
summary: Species-specific pest and vector control strategies, including the sterile insect technique, Wolbachia-based interventions, and genetic control technologies, offer powerful alternatives to broad-spectrum chemical control, with applications ranging from targeted crop protection to large-scale disease control. Among these, genetic control technologies are advancing rapidly, but the pace of technological development is outstripping the modelling tools needed to predict outcomes, guide technology design and its implementation, compare alternative strategies across different use settings, and support regulatory and operational decision-making. Here we present PesTwin, an agent-based modelling framework for simulating genetic control technologies across species, ecological settings, and deployment strategies within a common computational environment. PesTwin captures stochastic demographic effects, species-specific life-history traits, heterogeneous dispersal, and temporal variation in resource availability and infestation pressure. We validate PesTwin against published laboratory cage data from four genetic control systems, drawn from three studies, in two insect species, showing close agreement between predicted and observed population trajectories, including their replicate-to-replicate variability. We then illustrate how the same validated models extend beyond the cage to spatially explicit, field-scale scenarios, using PesTwin to explore how the timing, density and spatial placement of releases shape suppression and spread across heterogeneous landscapes. By making genetic control systems testable in silico before they are built or released, PesTwin can shorten the path from laboratory construct to field intervention: informing which constructs to prioritise, how to design the experiments that test them, where and when to release, and what evidence is needed to evaluate them.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **PesTwin**, a modular **agent-based modelling (ABM)** framework designed to simulate the release and impact of genetic control technologies on pest and vector populations.
- The architecture integrates **stochastic demographic effects**, species-specific life-history traits, and **heterogeneous dispersal** models to replicate complex ecological dynamics.
- It provides a unified computational environment for testing **in silico** interventions, including Wolbachia-based systems and sterile insect techniques, across diverse landscapes.
- The framework bridges the gap between laboratory results and field-scale implementation by allowing researchers to optimize **deployment strategies** and resource allocation.
key_results: Validated against published laboratory cage data from four unique genetic control systems.
Achieved close agreement between predicted and observed population trajectories across two insect species.
Successfully captured and replicated replicate-to-replicate variability in population dynamics.
Demonstrated the capability to scale models from laboratory cages to spatially explicit field-scale scenarios.
Identified critical thresholds for release timing, density, and spatial placement to maximize population suppression.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Oxford and Imperial researchers introduce PesTwin, an agent-based framework for simulating and optimizing genetic pest control interventions.

title: SVF-CR: Synchronized Visual-Facial Cross-Refinement for Multimodal Ambivalence and Hesitancy Recognition
summary: Ambivalence and hesitancy are subtle behavioral states that are expressed through a combination of verbal content, facial behavior, visual context, and acoustic cues. Effective recognition therefore requires not only extracting informative unimodal representations, but also modeling how temporally aligned behavioral evidence interacts across modalities. In this paper, we propose a synchronized visual-facial cross-refinement framework (SVF-CR) with pairwise multimodal evidence fusion for ambivalence and hesitancy recognition. The proposed method first extracts whole-video segment tokens and cropped-face segment tokens using the same temporal partition. The synchronized visual and facial tokens are refined through intra-modal self-attention and bidirectional visual-facial cross-attention, allowing whole-video context and local facial behavior to mutually refine each other before evidence construction. We then construct segment-level visual-facial evidence using consistency and discrepancy modeling, followed by temporal self-attention and attention pooling. Textual and acoustic features are lightly refined through context self-attention and are fused with the enhanced visual-facial evidence at the final decision stage using pairwise evidence fusion. Experiments on the BAH (Behavioral Ambivalence/Hesitancy) public evaluation split show that the proposed synchronized visual-facial cross-refinement improves public macro-F1 over both global visual-face token fusion and synchronized evidence baselines, achieving a public macro-F1 of 0.7156. Code is available at : https://github.com/hiinnnii/BAH-Challenge-ECCV2026\_SVF-CR.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **SVF-CR**, a framework designed to recognize subtle behavioral states by synchronizing whole-video segment tokens with local cropped-face tokens.
 • Employs **bidirectional visual-facial cross-attention** and intra-modal self-attention to allow contextual and local evidence to mutually refine one another.
 • Utilizes **consistency and discrepancy modeling** to construct segment-level evidence, which is then integrated with acoustic and textual data via pairwise fusion.
 • Addresses the challenge of **temporal alignment** in multimodal streams, specifically for detecting complex psychological states like ambivalence and hesitancy.
key_results: Achieved a public macro-F1 score of 0.7156 on the BAH evaluation split.
Outperformed baseline methods using global visual-face token fusion.
Validated that synchronized temporal partitioning significantly improves multimodal feature alignment.
Successfully integrated four distinct modalities: visual context, facial behavior, acoustic cues, and verbal content.
Released open-source code for the SVF-CR framework to support further research in behavioral recognition.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: The SVF-CR researchers introduce a synchronized cross-refinement framework that improves multimodal hesitancy detection by aligning facial and contextual tokens.

title: Two Vocabularies, One Phenomenon: Metadata Bias in AI Evidence Synthesis on Fertility Decline
summary:
Declining fertility is one of the defining policy questions of the next decade, and increasingly, what policymakers know about it is shaped by AI-synthesising the evidence base. But ask such a tool about reproduction and the answer depends on the word you use. The same phenomenon, framed clinically (e.g, infertility, IVF) or socially (e.g., childlessness, fertility intentions), is catalogued with radically different completeness. And the catalogue as much, if not more than the underlying scholarship, is what AI synthesis begins with (Bolaños et al., 2024). That databases under-index the social sciences, books, and grey literature is well established (Visser et al., 2021). What is new here is holding the topic fixed and asking whether metadata gaps act as a hidden policy filter on a single contested issue: the determinants of (in)fertility.
  We use two OpenAlex queries on the same phenomenon: a clinical basket (infertility, subfertility, ART, IVF, fecundity; n=101,645) and a social basket (childlessness, social infertility, fertility intentions, reproductive decision-making; n=3,646). We compare them on metadata completeness, open access, output type, and institutional provenance. The social framing is consistently less machine-legible: output skewed to books and dissertations, authorship university -- rather than healthcare-based. Open access rates are essentially equal (43.1% vs 41.3%), so the gap is in indexing depth, not paywalls, suggesting simple OA mandates will not fix it. On this same mixed literature, even before any coverage bias enters the picture, LLM tools already miss more than they catch when asked to extract hypotheses and claims (Uprety et al., 2025); the bias documented here compounds an already-imperfect extraction stage.

processed.summary:
- This research investigates **metadata bias** in AI-driven evidence synthesis, demonstrating how different linguistic framings (clinical vs. social) of the same topic result in disparate indexing completeness.
- The study identifies a critical **machine-legibility gap**, where social science literature (books and dissertations) is significantly under-indexed compared to clinical healthcare datasets in large-scale repositories.
- It highlights that **RAG-based systems** and LLM tools inherit systematic upstream biases from these metadata catalogues, which act as a hidden policy filter before the extraction phase even begins.
- Analysis shows that **indexing depth**, rather than Open Access availability, is the primary constraint, suggesting that standard OA mandates cannot rectify synthesis bias.
key_results: The clinical query returned 101,645 results compared to only 3,646 for the equivalent social science query.
Social science metadata is disproportionately skewed toward formats that are difficult for machines to parse, such as books.
Open Access rates are nearly identical (approx. 42%) between clinical and social baskets, proving the bias is not due to paywalls.
LLM extraction tools are found to miss more hypotheses and claims than they successfully catch in mixed literature samples.
Metadata gaps create a 'hidden filter' that leads AI synthesis tools to prioritize healthcare-based provenance over university-based social research.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers reveal that metadata indexing bias skews AI evidence synthesis, favoring clinical data over social science insights.

title: SYNRARE: Synthetic Rare Disease EHR Generation for ML Benchmarking
summary:
Motivation: Rare disease (RD) diagnosis is frequently delayed due to the similarities in symptoms to common disease variants. Machine Learning Algorithms applied to Electronic Health Records show promise for accelerating the diagnosis; however, legal and privacy concerns pose significant barriers. To address these issues, Synthetic Data Generation is an alternative method for obtaining Electronic Health Records and can be applied with any Machine Learning algorithm for benchmarking and development purposes. Despite the availability of Synthetic Data Generation algorithms, support for generating a subset of patients that differ in a definable degree from the majority to simulate patients with RD is often lacking.
  Results: We present SYNRARE, a graphical user interface based on the Synthea framework that enables easier modification and generation of synthetic Electronic Health Records of RD patients, which differ only to a definable degree from patients with common diseases, thereby enabling the benchmarking and testing of algorithms under controlled technical conditions. SYNRARE enables researchers to rapidly benchmark their Machine Learning algorithms across any scenario.
  Availability and implementation: SYNRARE, including detailed instructions for installing, is available at https://gitlab.sdu.dk/screen4care/synrare.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **SYNRARE**, a GUI-based framework built upon **Synthea** for generating synthetic **Electronic Health Records (EHR)** tailored for rare disease (RD) research.
* Enables the creation of patient cohorts that vary from common disease populations by a **definable degree**, facilitating more granular control over clinical simulations.
* Addresses data scarcity and privacy limitations in healthcare ML by providing a **benchmarking environment** for diagnostic algorithms without using sensitive real-world data.
* Simplifies the modification of complex medical event sequences to test the **robustness of ML models** in identifying subtle symptom deviations.
key_results: Developed a graphical user interface for easier manipulation of the Synthea framework.
Enabled precise control over the similarity between rare disease and common disease synthetic patients.
Provided a scalable solution for benchmarking ML algorithms in low-data medical regimes.
Eliminated privacy and legal barriers inherent in accessing real patient records for initial model development.
Released the tool as open-source software via GitLab for community benchmarking.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: University of Southern Denmark researchers launch SYNRARE to generate synthetic EHR data for benchmarking rare disease diagnostic algorithms.

title: System Capybara: Tracking Capabilities for Separation and Freshness (Extended Version)
summary:
Substructural type systems give strong static control over aliasing. Examples include uniqueness, separation, and borrowing. How can such control be brought to established languages whose programming models rely on higher-order abstraction, unrestricted aliasing, and pervasive sharing? We study this problem in the context of Scala. We show how to retrofit these guarantees selectively instead of globally: ordinary code keeps Scala's usual aliasing discipline, while stronger guarantees can be enforced where they matter.
  Our starting point is Scala's capture checking, whose treatment of capabilities is inspired by the object-capability tradition: capabilities are ordinary values, and capture sets record, in a value's type, which capabilities the value may use. We develop System Capybara, which adds a selective alias-control layer to this mechanism. By tracking separation, consumption, freshness, and read-only access for capabilities, Capybara recovers key reasoning principles from substructural and ownership-based disciplines without global invariants.
  We give a type-preserving translation from the surface calculus Capybara to CoreCapybara, a core calculus extending System Capless, the earlier foundation for capture checking. The translation uses quantifiers for capture polymorphism and freshness, and constraint-indexed modal types for separation. We prove a semantic soundness result for the core calculus in Lean 4 and derive type safety, memory safety (no use-after-free or double-free), immutability of read-only computations, and data-race freedom for well-typed programs.
  Finally, we implement Scala 3's new separation checker, which brings higher-order separation reasoning about effects, capabilities, and resources to ordinary Scala, including fearless concurrency.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **System Capybara**, a selective alias-control layer for Scala that retrofits **substructural guarantees** such as uniqueness, separation, and borrowing.
- Utilizes a translation from a surface calculus to **CoreCapybara**, which features **capture polymorphism**, freshness quantifiers, and constraint-indexed modal types.
- Provides a **Lean 4** soundness proof that ensures memory safety (no use-after-free), data-race freedom, and immutability of read-only computations.
- Implements a new **separation checker** in Scala 3, enabling higher-order reasoning about effects and resources for fearless concurrency.
key_results: Development of a selective substructural type system that avoids global invariants.
Formalization and semantic soundness proof of CoreCapybara using the Lean 4 proof assistant.
Elimination of common memory errors including use-after-free and double-free in a high-level language.
Successful implementation of separation tracking within the production Scala 3 compiler.
Achievement of data-race freedom for well-typed programs using capture checking.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: EPFL researchers introduce System Capybara, enabling selective substructural type guarantees and fearless concurrency within the Scala 3 compiler.

title: Spectrally Deconfounded Gradient Boosting
summary: Flexible machine-learning methods can be sensitive to hidden confounding: they may learn associations induced by unobserved confounders rather than stable signals. Spectral deconfounding mitigates this problem by shrinking high-variance directions of the covariate matrix that, under dense confounding, carry latent confounder information. Existing work has largely focused on linear models. We develop a nonlinear spectral deconfounding framework for gradient boosting. Our approach replaces the ordinary squared-error loss by a spectral loss, which alters the boosting dynamics by slowing down learning in confounding-aligned directions. We show that deconfounding is not achieved by the spectral loss alone, but by the interaction between spectral shrinkage and regularization, especially in terms of early stopping. Moreover, we provide a mixed-model interpretation that connects LAVA-type shrinkage to random-effects adjustment and yields an empirical-Bayes procedure for tuning the spectral loss. We also extend the method to general likelihoods and nonlinear confounding using Laplace approximations and kernel random effects. Across synthetic and real-world experiments, spectrally deconfounded boosting improves estimation of the target function under hidden confounding and is substantially more scalable than existing nonlinear spectral deconfounding baselines.

processed.summary:
• Introduces a **nonlinear spectral deconfounding framework** specifically for gradient boosting to mitigate the impact of unobserved confounders.
• Replaces standard squared-error loss with a **spectral loss**, which penalizes learning in high-variance covariate directions likely to harbor latent confounding.
• Utilizes a **mixed-model interpretation** to facilitate automated hyperparameter tuning via an empirical-Bayes procedure.
• Supports **general likelihoods and nonlinear confounding** through the integration of Laplace approximations and kernel random effects.
key_results: Significantly reduces bias from hidden confounding compared to standard gradient boosting models.
Provides a more scalable solution for nonlinear deconfounding than previous kernel-based baselines.
Establishes that deconfounding emerges from the interaction of spectral shrinkage and early stopping.
Demonstrates improved target function estimation on both synthetic datasets and real-world benchmarks.
Enables effective tuning of the spectral loss through a rigorous empirical-Bayes framework.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce spectrally deconfounded gradient boosting to mitigate hidden confounding bias and improve predictive stability.

title: Diversifying to Verify: When Task-Equivalent Programs Differ in Verifiability
summary:
Program verification is crucial for software correctness, but producing fully verified programs remains difficult in practice. This paper studies whether implementation structure affects automated verifiability when multiple generated programs are intended to satisfy the same task-level semantics.
  We present Diversify2Verify, a staged LLM-based pipeline for Why3 that infers representation-specific contracts, generates and tests diverse recursive and imperative array/list implementations, and attempts verification with bounded verifier-guided annotation repair. We also construct a verification-oriented benchmark of 73 tasks over integers, arrays, and lists, yielding 292 implementation variants. Diversify2Verify verifies 96 artifacts initially and 154 after two repair passes, improving artifact-level verification from 32.9% to 52.7%. At the task level, at least one variant verifies for 49 of 73 tasks, a 67.1% success rate. These results show that task-equivalent implementations can differ substantially in verifiability and that implementation diversity helps find verification-friendly artifacts.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **Diversify2Verify**, a staged LLM-based pipeline designed for the **Why3** platform to bridge the gap between code generation and formal verification.
- Utilizes a strategy of generating **diverse task-equivalent implementations** (recursive, imperative, array-based, list-based) to identify the most verification-friendly structure.
- Incorporates **bounded verifier-guided annotation repair** to fix contracts and invariants based on feedback from the formal verifier.
- Demonstrates that the **implementation structure** of functionally equivalent code significantly dictates whether automated tools can successfully prove correctness.
key_results: Initial verification of 96 artifacts increased to 154 after two repair passes.
Artifact-level verification success rate improved from 32.9% to 52.7%.
Achieved a 67.1% task-level success rate, meaning at least one implementation verified for 49 of 73 tasks.
The benchmark contains 73 distinct tasks resulting in 292 unique implementation variants.
Diversity in code implementation proved more effective for verification than attempting to repair a single implementation.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers show that generating diverse code implementations significantly increases the probability of successful formal verification by LLMs.

title: Quantifying nanoparticle size effect on the photoacoustic generation efficiency
summary: Photoacoustic (PA) signal generation in colloidal suspensions of optically absorbing nanoparticles is dominated by the thermal expansion of water for gold nanoparticles, but remains mostly unexplored for organic nanoparticles. Here, we derive a model where the PA generation efficiency scales with particle size and thermoelastic contrast with water. The model is validated using solid lipid nanoparticles labeled with several BODIPY dyes. This experimental validation paves the way for quantitative PA characterization of nanomaterials and rational design of PA contrast agents.

processed.summary:
• Derives a mathematical model to quantify **photoacoustic (PA) generation efficiency** in colloidal suspensions, emphasizing the scaling relationship with particle size.
• Investigates the **thermoelastic contrast** between nanoparticles and water, identifying key differences between metallic (gold) and organic absorbers.
• Validates the theoretical framework using **solid lipid nanoparticles** labeled with BODIPY dyes, enabling a predictive approach to nanomaterial design.
• Provides a foundation for the **rational design** of organic photoacoustic contrast agents in biomedical imaging applications.
key_results: Confirmed that PA generation efficiency scales directly with nanoparticle size.
Established thermoelastic contrast with the surrounding medium as a primary driver of signal strength.
Demonstrated that organic nanoparticles follow distinct signal generation patterns compared to gold nanoparticles.
Successfully validated the derived model using dye-labeled solid lipid nanoparticles.
Paved the way for quantitative characterization of nanomaterials via photoacoustic techniques.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a model quantifying how nanoparticle size and thermoelastic properties dictate photoacoustic efficiency for improved contrast agents.

title: Scale-Bridging Phase-Field Modeling of Microstructure Evolution by FE$^2$ Computational Homogenization
summary:
Phase-field models have become a standard tool for simulating complex microstructure evolution in materials, but their application to engineering-scale components is often hindered by prohibitive computational costs arising from the need to resolve fine-scale features. To address this challenge, we propose a consistent homogenization framework for phase-field theory. By enforcing a Hill-Mandel-type condition of micro-homogeneity formulated in terms of Gurtin's microforces, a well-posed boundary value problem is derived for the representative volume element (RVE), establishing rigorous micro-macro relations for both the order parameter and its gradient.
  The theory is implemented within a computational two-scale (FE$^2$) scheme and validated against direct numerical simulations. Two distinct examples are investigated: a minimal Allen-Cahn model and a mechanically-coupled model for stress-driven martensitic phase transformation. The results demonstrate that the proposed framework can reliably predict the spatial and temporal evolution of the macroscopically averaged fields with reasonable accuracy.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **consistent homogenization framework** for phase-field theory to bridge the gap between fine-scale microstructure evolution and engineering-scale components.
- Utilizes a **Hill-Mandel-type micro-homogeneity condition** formulated through Gurtin's microforces to establish rigorous micro-macro relations for order parameters.
- Implements a **two-scale (FE2) computational scheme** that solves boundary value problems on a Representative Volume Element (RVE) to determine macroscopic behavior.
- Enables the simulation of **stress-driven martensitic phase transformations** and Allen-Cahn models with significantly reduced computational overhead compared to direct numerical simulations.
key_results: Derivation of a well-posed boundary value problem for the Representative Volume Element (RVE).
Establishment of rigorous micro-macro relations for both the order parameter and its gradient.
Validation of the FE2 scheme against direct numerical simulations (DNS) for accuracy.
Successful application to a mechanically-coupled model for martensitic phase transformation.
Reliable prediction of the spatial and temporal evolution of macroscopically averaged fields.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop an FE2 computational homogenization framework to efficiently scale phase-field microstructure simulations for engineering-scale material analysis.

title: DKCD: Domain Knowledge-Enhanced Causal Discovery from Unstructured Data
summary: Causal discovery from unstructured data is a challenging yet underexplored task in high-expertise domains such as healthcare, finance, and education. Existing methods typically leverage the general knowledge of large language models (LLMs) to identify causal factors from unstructured data and annotate them into structured data for causal graph construction. However, they remain limited by two key challenges (CHs): (CH1) insufficient identification of latent factors, which are implicit in the data yet essential for causal discovery, due to the lack of domain-specific knowledge; and (CH2) unreliable factor annotation, caused by the lack of domain-grounded reasoning, which propagates errors to the resulting causal graphs. To address these challenges, we introduce a novel Domain Knowledge-enhanced Causal Discovery framework (DKCD) for causal discovery from unstructured data in high-expertise domains with three interconnected components: (1) Knowledge Mining: It retrieves relevant domain knowledge based on observable factors to support subsequent causal reasoning. (2) Knowledge-guided Causal Reasoning: Reasoning with relevant knowledge, it discovers latent causal factors to address CH1 and generates key causal clues for more accurate data annotation to address CH2. (3) Causal Structure Discovery: It constructs the final causal graphs based on a more complete factor set and accurate annotations. Experiments on two domain-specific datasets show that DKCD significantly improves both causal factor identification and causal graph construction.

processed.summary:
• The **DKCD framework** addresses the limitations of LLMs in causal discovery by integrating **Domain Knowledge Mining** to retrieve context-specific insights from unstructured data.
• It utilizes a **Knowledge-guided Causal Reasoning** component to identify **latent factors** and generate causal clues, mitigating errors in data annotation for complex domains.
• The architecture employs a **RAG-based approach** to ground LLM reasoning in domain-specific expertise, ensuring more reliable and complete causal factor sets.
• By combining structured knowledge retrieval with LLM reasoning, the system builds more accurate **causal graphs** compared to vanilla LLM annotation methods.
key_results: Significantly improves causal factor identification in high-expertise domains like healthcare and finance.
Effectively identifies latent variables that are implicit in unstructured datasets.
Reduces error propagation in causal graph construction through domain-grounded reasoning.
Outperforms existing baselines on two domain-specific benchmarking datasets.
Demonstrates the efficacy of using RAG to bridge the gap between general LLM knowledge and specialized expertise.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce DKCD, a framework using domain-specific RAG to enhance causal discovery and graph construction from unstructured data.

title: Towards Detecting Inconsistencies in End-to-end Generated TODs
summary: Generative AI is profoundly transforming the core technologies behind conversational systems, shifting from component-based to end-to-end approaches. However, Large Language Models (LLMs) may still generate inconsistencies, a critical issue particularly in Task-Oriented Dialogues (TODs), where system responses must strictly adhere to information from a domain knowledge base (e.g., restaurants in a city). A single hallucination (e.g., suggesting a non-existent restaurant) can lead to severe task failures. We investigate a method for automatically detecting inconsistencies by conceptualizing TODs as a Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP), where variables represent dialogue segments referencing the conversational domain, and constraints among variables capture dialogue properties such as turn coherence and adherence to domain knowledge. We propose a pipeline that first identifies variables in a target dialogue and then applies a CSP solver to identify valid solutions. By comparing the target dialogue with valid variable assignments, we can detect inconsistencies and suggest minimal changes to ensure dialogue consistency. We demonstrate the high accuracy of the CSP-based approach in detecting inconsistencies, and provide a detailed analysis of our findings.

processed.summary:
* Proposes a novel framework for **hallucination detection** in Task-Oriented Dialogues (TODs) by modeling conversational data as a **Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)**.
* The architecture utilizes a **pipeline approach** to extract variables from dialogue segments and applies a CSP solver to enforce **domain knowledge** and **turn coherence**.
* Facilitates automated **error correction** by identifying minimal necessary changes to align generated responses with valid variable assignments from the solver.
key_results: Achieved high accuracy in identifying factual inconsistencies in end-to-end TODs.
Developed a method to model turn coherence and domain adherence as mathematical constraints.
Enabled the detection of domain-specific hallucinations such as non-existent restaurant entities.
Provided a mechanism for suggesting minimal dialogue edits to ensure consistency with a knowledge base.
Demonstrated that CSP-based validation effectively supplements generative models in task-critical applications.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers apply Constraint Satisfaction Programming to detect and fix hallucinations in end-to-end generative task-oriented dialogues.

title: Communication-Efficient Digital-Twin Coordination for Heterogeneous LLM Embodied Agents over Computing Power Networks
summary: Embodied agent teams powered by heterogeneous large language models (LLMs) are being widely deployed in physical artificial intelligence such as smart factories, warehouses, and service robotics. To enable collaboration among such an agent team, efficient coordination mechanisms that operate reliably under limited network resources are required. However, existing heterogeneous LLM-agent coordination frameworks that rely on multi-round natural-language-based conversations introduce three coupled challenges. First, inter-agent dialogue incurs communication overhead that grows rapidly with team size. Second, the quality of coordination is constrained by the heterogeneous capabilities of the agent team's LLMs. Third, agents may suffer from action delays due to iterative negotiation. To address these challenges, we propose LDT-Coord, a networked coordination framework built upon a lightweight digital twin (DT). Specifically, each agent independently selects its intended action and reports both the action decision and a structured temporal constraint over shared resources to the DT server, thereby decoupling coordination performance from natural-language reasoning ability. Then, DT executes a training-free, rule-based orchestrator algorithm to resolve cross-agent conflicts and returns coordination instructions to prevent such conflicts. To further reduce communication overhead, we formulate agent reporting control as a constrained partially observable Markov decision process (C-POMDP) and solve it with the PPO-Lagrangian algorithm. Simulation results show that LDT-Coord achieves a task success rate comparable to conventional coordination methods while reducing communication overhead by more than 70x and maintaining robustness under LLM heterogeneity.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **LDT-Coord**, a communication-efficient framework for coordinating **heterogeneous LLM embodied agents** using a centralized **lightweight digital twin (DT)**.
- Replaces bandwidth-intensive **multi-round natural language negotiation** with structured reports of action decisions and temporal constraints to decouple coordination from LLM reasoning limits.
- Features a **rule-based orchestrator** to resolve cross-agent conflicts and utilizes a **C-POMDP** model with **PPO-Lagrangian** to optimize agent reporting frequency.
- Addresses performance bottlenecks in **computing power networks** by reducing communication overhead while maintaining high task success rates in smart factory and robotic scenarios.
key_results: Achieved over 70x reduction in communication overhead compared to natural-language-based coordination.
Maintained task success rates comparable to standard multi-round negotiation methods.
Eliminated action delays caused by iterative LLM reasoning and dialogue cycles.
Demonstrated high robustness against model heterogeneity (varying LLM sizes/capabilities).
Successfully optimized reporting control under limited network resources using PPO-Lagrangian.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce LDT-Coord, a digital-twin framework that slashes LLM agent coordination communication by 70x using structured reporting.

title: Subexponential Algorithm for High Multiplicity Fair Division of Mixed Instances via Stereometry
summary: We study the problem of computing an envy-free (EF) allocation of $m$ indivisible items among $n$ agents when items come in three distinct types. Each agent holds additive valuations over item types that may be positive (goods), negative (chores), or mixed. We present the first subexponential-time algorithm with running time time $(n \cdot m)^{O(\sqrt{n})}$ that finds an EF allocation whenever one exists, or correctly reports that none exists. Our approach exploits a geometric representation of EF allocations as convex polyhedra in $\mathbb{R}^3$ and applies Miller's planar cycle-separator theorem to recursively decompose the agent set into balanced subgroups. We further extend the algorithm to handle agents whose allocations are fixed in advance, preserving envy-freeness across all agents.

processed.summary:
- Introduces the first **subexponential-time algorithm** for computing envy-free (EF) allocations of indivisible items among agents with mixed (positive and negative) valuations.
- Utilizes a **geometric representation** that maps allocations to convex polyhedra in 3D space, allowing for structural analysis of item distribution.
- Employs **Miller's planar cycle-separator theorem** to recursively decompose agent sets into balanced subgroups, optimizing the search space for valid allocations.
- Addresses the **high multiplicity** problem where many items belong to a small number of distinct types, ensuring the algorithm scales efficiently.
key_results: Achieves a subexponential running time of (n * m)^O(sqrt(n)) for the envy-free allocation problem.
First algorithm to handle 'mixed' instances containing both goods and chores with high multiplicity.
Guarantees finding an envy-free allocation if one exists or provides a proof of non-existence.
Successfully extends the geometric framework to handle constraints where specific agent allocations are fixed in advance.
Provides a theoretical bridge between discrete fair division and 3D stereometric decomposition.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop a subexponential algorithm using stereometry to solve complex envy-free allocation problems for mixed item types.

title: Letting the Data Speak: Extracting Keywords from Crowdsourced Collections with AI
summary: Identifying and assigning keywords at scale is a technical, practical, and ethical challenge for crowdsourced collections. This article reports the findings of the "Extracting Keywords from Crowdsourced Collections" project, which used the Their Finest Hour Online Archive, a crowdsourced Second World War digital collection hosted by the University of Oxford, as a case study. The project evaluated three Natural Language Processing approaches to automate keyword extraction: Named Entity Recognition, Keyword Extraction, and Topic Modelling. It tested these approaches across a range of artificial intelligence techniques, from traditional statistical methods to modern GenAI neural networks. Our quantitative and qualitative findings indicate that Natural Language Processing approaches offer real potential for keyword extraction at scale in crowdsourced collections, but that no single method offers a complete solution and that model choice significantly shapes results. We argue that in crowdsourced collections, where metadata is the direct product of engagement with living contributors, automated keyword extraction raises distinct stewardship responsibilities that must be addressed alongside technical performance. Open-weight, extractive models emerge from our evaluation as best placed to support responsible deployment, while generative AI, despite its abstractive potential, introduces accountability risks that anyone managing crowdsourced collections should weigh carefully.

processed.summary:
- Evaluated three NLP paradigms—**Named Entity Recognition (NER)**, **Keyword Extraction**, and **Topic Modelling**—applied to a large-scale Second World War digital archive.
- Benchmarked **traditional statistical methods** against **modern Generative AI (GenAI)** neural networks to automate metadata generation and archival indexing.
- Identified that **open-weight, extractive models** provide superior accountability and transparency for crowdsourced metadata compared to abstractive generative models.
- Analyzed the critical tradeoff between **technical performance** and **stewardship responsibilities**, emphasizing the risks of hallucination in generative workflows for historical data.
key_results: No single NLP method provides a comprehensive, one-size-fits-all solution for crowdsourced keyword extraction.
Model architecture selection (extractive vs. generative) fundamentally shapes the qualitative nature of the resulting metadata.
Open-weight models are identified as the most suitable for responsible deployment in sensitive public archives.
Generative AI models introduce significant accountability risks due to their abstractive tendencies and potential for misinformation.
Automated approaches provide a scalable pathway for processing massive, diverse crowdsourced collections that manual archival cannot handle.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: University of Oxford researchers demonstrate that open-weight extractive models outperform generative AI for responsible historical archive metadata generation.

title: Effects of Robotic Touch on Older Users During Walking Guidance by a Humanoid Robot
summary: The shortage of healthcare staff is a challenge in geriatric care. To address this, robots can be integrated into care settings to provide assistance and emotional support. A promising application is walking guidance, particularly benefiting older adults as navigation skills deteriorate with aging. As walking guidance involves direct contact, the aim of this study is to understand how older adults perceive and respond to different touch modes during guided walking. 24 older adults (68 - 88 yrs.) walked four times a ten-meter trajectory guided by the robot TIAGo Pro in four contact conditions: no physical contact (NC); physical contact through holding the robot's wrist with the hand (HH); physical interaction through linking arms with the robot (LA); and physical contact through resting the forearm on the robots forearm (FC). A multimodal assessment approach included electrocardiogram, electrodermal activity, contact force, distance to robot, and questionnaires. Physiological results reveal a slight increase in stress levels during robot interaction. Behavioural and subjective measures, however, show overall acceptance of robotic touch. The two conditions corresponding to larger interaction forces (HH and FC) were associated with lower relative distances between participant and robot, indicating a higher trust and confidence. Questionnaire responses supported these findings, evidencing greater perceived safety, trust and comfort in these conditions. This study provides insights for the design of robotic walking guidance assistance, indicating that gentle, stable touch is preferred by older adults in comparison to contactless interaction.

processed.summary:
- Evaluation of **multimodal HRI (Human-Robot Interaction)** frameworks using the **TIAGo Pro** humanoid robot for geriatric walking assistance.
- Investigation of four distinct **physical contact modes** (NC, HH, LA, FC) to measure physiological and behavioral responses in older adults.
- Implementation of a sensor suite including **ECG, electrodermal activity (EDA), and contact force sensors** to quantify trust, stress, and user proximity.
- Findings demonstrate that **stable physical interaction** reduces interpersonal distance and improves perceived safety compared to contactless navigation.
key_results: Physical contact conditions (HH and FC) significantly reduced the relative distance between the user and the robot.
Physiological data showed a slight increase in stress levels during all robotic interactions regardless of contact mode.
Subjective assessments indicated higher perceived safety and comfort during 'Holding Wrist' and 'Forearm Contact' trials.
Higher interaction forces were positively correlated with increased user trust and confidence.
The study confirms that older adults prefer gentle, stable touch over contactless guidance for navigation assistance.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that physical touch from humanoid robots significantly improves navigation trust and safety for older adults.

title: LongMedBench: Benchmarking Medical Agents for Long-Horizon Clinical Decision-Making
summary: In this work, we introduce LongMedBench, a real-world EHR-based benchmark for long-horizon clinical decision-making. Prior evaluations of LLM-based medical agents have largely emphasized short-context knowledge QA and tool use. However, real-world medical care is inherently longitudinal, and clinicians must aggregate evidence across repeated visits, tests, and evolving treatments. Therefore, long-horizon interaction is essential for realistic assessment. LongMedBench is constructed via a reproducible pipeline that integrates MIMIC-IV admission records and clinical notes into time-series event streams and long-context memory datasets, enabling long-horizon, multi-session interactions between agents and a clinical environment. It comprises 335 patients, with 19.72 inpatient visits per patient on average and 44.91 medical events per visit. Guided by the long-horizon decision process, we propose an evaluation taxonomy with three suites: fact-based QA, temporal reasoning, and long-horizon decision-making. This taxonomy measures how agents understand and leverage historical patient information over extended horizons. Our experiments show that while recent LLMs can make good use of explicit timestamps, they have challenges in implicit time inference; The RAG and agent memory system can improve the performance of information retrieval tasks, but the performance of decision-making tasks is highly dependent on the model's immediate context.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **LongMedBench**, a novel benchmark designed to evaluate LLM agents on **long-horizon clinical decision-making** using real-world longitudinal EHR data.
- Features a **reproducible pipeline** that transforms MIMIC-IV records into time-series event streams and long-context memory datasets for multi-session interaction.
- Proposes a three-tier evaluation taxonomy—**fact-based QA**, **temporal reasoning**, and **long-horizon decision-making**—to measure how agents aggregate evidence over time.
- Highlights that while **RAG and memory systems** improve retrieval, high-stakes decision-making remains heavily dependent on the model's **immediate context window**.
key_results: LongMedBench utilizes 335 patient profiles with an average of 19.72 visits and 44.91 events per visit.
Current LLMs demonstrate competency with explicit timestamps but fail significantly at implicit time inference.
Information retrieval tasks show performance gains when using RAG and dedicated agent memory systems.
Clinical decision-making performance is bottlenecked by immediate context rather than historical memory access.
The benchmark successfully simulates realistic multi-session interactions between agents and evolving clinical environments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce LongMedBench to evaluate medical agents on long-horizon clinical decision-making and longitudinal patient records.

title: Automatic Thematic Indexing of Large Literary Corpora: A Machine Learning Approach to Voltaire's Complete Works
summary: Thematic indexing -- the practice of assigning structured conceptual labels to sections of text -- is essential to scholarly access in large-scale literary and historical editions, yet it remains a largely manual, labour-intensive process. This paper explores the application of machine learning to automatic thematic indexing, using two substantial sub-corpora of the Complete Works of Voltaire as a test case: the Essai sur les mœurs et l'esprit des nations and the Questions sur l'Encyclopédie. The task is framed as a multi-label classification problem, in which a model must assign the set of index entries that a professional indexer would apply to a given page of text. We compare a range of approaches -- from encoder-based models with classification heads to generative large language models (LLMs) fine-tuned via Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) -- spanning model sizes from approximately 3 to 120 billion parameters. Our best-performing model, from the Mistral family in a 4-bit quantised configuration, achieves F1 scores of up to 0.67; we argue that these figures represent lower bounds, given the inherent subjectivity of professional indexing and the frequency with which model predictions prove semantically valid despite diverging from the print index. We further evaluate cross-corpus generalisation and conduct a detailed qualitative analysis of model behaviour on literary and rhetorical features of the source texts that prove particularly resistant to automated treatment. Our findings have implications for the broader challenge of providing structured thematic access to large-scale literary and historical corpora.

processed.summary:
- The study addresses **thematic indexing** by framing it as a **multi-label classification** problem for large-scale historical and literary editions.
- Researchers compared several architectures, including **encoder-based models** and **generative LLMs** (Mistral family) ranging from 3B to 120B parameters.
- The methodology utilizes **Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)** and **4-bit quantization** to fine-tune models on domain-specific, high-complexity French literary texts.
- The approach highlights the challenges of **AI evaluation** in subjective domains, suggesting that traditional metrics like F1 represent a lower bound of actual utility.
key_results: Mistral-based generative models achieved the highest F1 scores of 0.67.
Generative LLMs outperformed traditional encoder-based models with classification heads.
Models demonstrated successful cross-corpus generalization between different literary sub-corpora.
Quantized 4-bit configurations remained highly effective for niche scholarly classification tasks.
Qualitative analysis showed model predictions are often semantically valid even when they fail to match exact ground-truth indices.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that fine-tuned Mistral models effectively automate thematic indexing for large-scale historical literary corpora using LoRA.

title: Robot Trajectron V3: A Probabilistic Shared Control Framework for SE(3) Manipulation
summary: We aim to address the challenge of teleoperating robotic arms for high-degree-of-freedom (high-DoF) manipulation tasks, which is cognitively demanding and error-prone, particularly when relying on low-bandwidth interfaces. We propose Robot Trajectron V3 (RT-V3), a probabilistic shared control framework designed for $SE(3)$ grasping tasks. RT-V3 formulates shared control as Bayesian inference by learning a prior over user intent and combining it with real-time user commands to estimate the posterior intent distribution. The prior models user intent as a distribution over future trajectories conditioned on past robot dynamics and visual scene context. The intent prior is parameterized by a transformer-based conditional generative model that reasons over point clouds and candidate grasp poses, together with a factorized translation-rotation representation that improves learning efficiency in high-dimensional action spaces.
